original_abstract:
This paper examines the integration of Central and Eastern European (CEE) nations into global value chains (GVCs) and evaluates the resulting implications for their long-term economic advancement. While these economies have achieved significant industrial growth and high export volumes in medium- and high-tech sectors, the authors argue that this progress may be a "statistical illusion" if the domestic contribution to value added remains low.

By utilizing the World Input-Output Database (WIOD) to calculate participation and position indices, the study demonstrates that CEE countries have become deeply integrated into GVCs, primarily as assembly hubs or "factories" for foreign lead firms. The empirical analysis reveals a decoupling between gross export dynamics and actual value-added gains. While CEE nations increasingly specialize in technology-intensive products in terms of trade volume, the high-value functions—such as R&D, design, and marketing—often remain concentrated in "headquarter economies."

The authors suggest that this functional dependency poses a risk of the "middle-income trap," where rising labor costs and a lack of indigenous innovation stifle further convergence with Western Europe. To "climb the economic league table," the paper calls for a strategic shift in public policy. It advocates for an industrial upgrading framework that moves beyond sector-based interventions toward supporting the functional advancement of domestic firms. Key recommendations include fostering local technological competencies, incentivizing R&D that transcends simple imitation, and implementing proactive industrial policies designed to rebalance the asymmetrical relationships between local suppliers and multinational corporations.
humanized_abstract:
The paper examines the CEE countries’ integration into the global value chains (GVCs) and the implications of this new development on their long-term growth potential. The CEE countries have experienced a fast-paced development of the industry and record high export volumes in medium- and high-tech products. However, the increase in industry value added is often a statistical illusion and the domestic value added share is often very low.

The paper applies participation and position indices derived from the World Input-Output Database (WIOD) to reveal the involvement of CEE countries in Global Value Chains (GVCs). Our results document that all CEE countries are deeply engaged in GVCs and generally perform tasks that reflect their new role in the global economy as low-skilled production locations — or “factories” — serving lead firms often located in other regions of the world. An analysis of the relationship between the dynamics of total exports and value added realisation of CEE countries uncovers a pattern of a pronounced decoupling of these two variables. Although the technology content of exports supplied by CEE countries has increased in the recent period, the high skill-bias in the linkages created in GVCs tends to work to the advantage of the “headquarter economies”.

The authors find that the E.U. enlarged has a functional dependency with Western Europe that increases the risk of the “middle-income trap”. High labor costs and lack of endogenous innovations are insufficient to drive the necessary adjustment to overcome the functional dependency and for the E.U. enlarged countries to “climb the economic league table”. The paper therefore calls for a change in policy composition with the objective of moving to an “industrial upgrading” policy framework. This policy framework goes beyond past sector based policy interventions by focusing more on the functional capacity of the indigenous firms. To achieve these changes the paper calls for developing endogenously the required technological skills to absorb new technological developments and the restructuring of R&D activities from mere adaptation and imitation to more innovative functions and for more proactive and symmetrical industrial policies that address the increasing imbalances in the relations between indigenous companies and multinationals in the enlarged EU.
